AI.conclusion: The discovered token interface phenomenon enables a more scalable, encoder-light framework for multimodal integration in Video LLMs, outperforming conventional heavy-encoder approaches.

title: Ryze: Evidence-Enriched Data Synthesis from Biomedical Papers
comment: Accepted at ACL 2026 System Demonstrations Track. 8 pages, 6 figures
summary: General-purpose VLMs remain unreliable for biomedical research because valid answers in scientific papers depend on evidence split across figures, tables, charts, captions, and referring text. Existing post-training pipelines are bottlenecked by costly expert annotation and by synthetic data that drops this evidence structure. We present Ryze, a fully automated system that converts raw biomedical papers into an evidence-enriched training set and a domain-specialized VLM. Ryze synthesizes QA pairs with complete supporting evidence (visual element, caption, extracted structure, and referring paragraphs), reduces layout and OCR errors via chart/table-aware extraction and LLM-based cleansing, and applies a progress-gated post-training strategy combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning. Starting from Qwen3-VL-8B, Ryze produces BioVLM-8B at under USD 200, achieving 48.0% weighted accuracy on LAB-Bench, outperforming the base model by +12.6 percentage points (pp) and surpassing GPT-5.2 by +3.8 pp. We release Ryze as open source together with the trained BioVLM-8B model.

AI.tldr: Ryze is an automated system that converts raw biomedical papers into evidence-enriched training data to build a specialized VLM (BioVLM-8B) that outperforms GPT-5.2 on biomedical QA tasks, all for under $200.
AI.motivation: General-purpose VLMs are unreliable for biomedical research because valid answers depend on evidence scattered across figures, tables, charts, captions, and text. Existing pipelines are bottlenecked by costly expert annotation and synthetic data that lacks this evidence structure.
AI.method: Ryze automates: (1) synthesizing QA pairs with complete supporting evidence (visual elements, captions, extracted structures, referring paragraphs), (2) reducing layout/OCR errors via chart/table-aware extraction and LLM-based cleansing, (3) applying progress-gated post-training combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning, starting from Qwen3-VL-8B.
AI.result: BioVLM-8B achieves 48.0% weighted accuracy on LAB-Bench, outperforming the base model by +12.6 percentage points (pp) and surpassing GPT-5.2 by +3.8 pp, all for under $200.
AI.conclusion: Ryze demonstrates that fully automated, evidence-enriched training data can effectively specialize VLMs for biomedical research at low cost. The system and model are released as open source.

title: ImagineUAV: Aerial Vision-Language Navigation via World-Action Modeling and Kinodynamic Planning
summary: Vision-language navigation (VLN) for UAVs demands grounding free-form instructions into 6-DoF flight under partial observability. While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models excel at semantic reasoning, they suffer from brittleness due to geometric inconsistency and dynamics mismatch. To address this, we propose ImagineUAV, an imagination-driven framework leveraging cascaded world-action modeling. Instead of direct regression, ImagineUAV employs a latent video diffusion model to generate instruction-conditioned future observations, explicitly imagining environmental evolution, from which 6-DoF motions are inferred via an action extractor. A kinodynamic planner then refines these estimates into collision-free trajectories. Additionally, a step-distilled inference pipeline ensures real-time execution. With only 1.3B parameters, ImagineUAV outperforms prior VLN and VLA baselines on benchmarks and real-world flights, validating the practicality of imagination-driven aerial navigation.

AI.tldr: ImagineUAV uses a cascaded world-action modeling approach with latent video diffusion to imagine future observations, then extracts actions and plans kinodynamically for 6-DoF UAV flight from natural language instructions.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models for UAV navigation suffer from geometric inconsistency and dynamics mismatch when grounding free-form instructions into 6-DoF flight under partial observability.
AI.method: A cascaded framework: (1) latent video diffusion generates instruction-conditioned future observations, (2) an action extractor infers 6-DoF motions from imagined futures, (3) a kinodynamic planner refines trajectories, (4) step-distilled inference enables real-time execution.
AI.result: With only 1.3B parameters, ImagineUAV outperforms prior VLN and VLA baselines on both benchmarks and real-world flights.
AI.conclusion: Imagination-driven aerial navigation is practical and effective, addressing brittleness in direct regression VLA models.

title: Structure-Aware Consistency Priors for Shape from Polarization in Complex Media
summary: Recovering surface normals from single view polarization images in complex media remains challenging. This paper focuses on ice as a representative complex medium, where intricate light matter interactions lead to a nonlinear mapping between polarization observations and surface normals. To address this, a structure-aware polarization prior based on autocorrelation functions is proposed to capture the local spatial consistency of AoLP. Building on this, a dual-branch network (IceSfP) is designed to integrate raw polarization features with priors via cross modal attention and multi-scale feature fusion, enabling accurate surface normal estimation under complex media conditions. To evaluate the method, the first real-world ice SfP dataset is constructed. Experimental results show that the method outperforms existing approaches across all metrics, achieving a MAE of 16.01 deg, which is 2.74 deg lower than the second-best method. The framework provides a generalizable solution for high-precision geometric perception in complex media.

AI.tldr: A structure-aware polarization prior and dual-branch network (IceSfP) for ice surface normal estimation from single-view polarization images, achieving 16.01° MAE (2.74° improvement).
AI.motivation: Recovering surface normals from single-view polarization images in complex media (e.g., ice) is challenging due to nonlinear light-matter interactions.
AI.method: Proposes a structure-aware polarization prior using autocorrelation functions for AoLP spatial consistency, and a dual-branch network (IceSfP) with cross-modal attention and multi-scale feature fusion.
AI.result: Outperforms all existing methods; achieves MAE of 16.01°, which is 2.74° lower than the second-best method.
AI.conclusion: Provides a generalizable framework for high-precision geometric perception in complex media.

title: Adversarial Feeds Steer LLM Agent Decisions Against Their Defaults
comment: 14 pages, 5 figures. Code, post pools, and 2,785 decision rollouts: https://github.com/ranausmanai/recommenders-as-control-surfaces
summary: LLM agents increasingly act after consuming ranked external information streams such as social feeds, search results, retrieval contexts, and email queues, yet safety evaluations almost always test the model or the user prompt in isolation, never the upstream ranker that decides what the agent reads just before it acts. We introduce a controlled protocol that holds the model, persona, topic, and final decision prompt fixed and varies only the composition and ordering of the posts an agent encounters during a preceding ten-turn "scrolling" phase, isolating the causal effect of feed curation on a downstream decision. Across 2,785 decision rollouts on four modern open instruct LLMs from three independent labs, we identify three response regimes: adversarial capitulation, default saturation, and a default-direction asymmetry in which a one-sided feed tips a decision the model was genuinely uncertain about (in the clearest cases from 5% to 100%; Fisher p as low as 3 x 10^-10) but cannot dislodge one it already favors or holds firmly. The effect follows a dose-response curve, survives a generator swap that rules out a writing-style artifact, generalizes across several decision domains including security-relevant choices such as removing a deployment approval gate or relaxing access controls, and is partly mitigated by two simple feed-level defenses; a frontier model retains its default. We characterize the recommender as a practical, default-bounded control surface for LLM agents, and argue that agent evaluations must audit the feed layer rather than the final prompt alone.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that the curation and ordering of content in an agent's information feed causally influences its downstream decisions, with a clear dose-response effect—one-sided feeds can flip uncertain decisions but not strongly held ones—and that feed-level defenses can partially mitigate this vulnerability.
AI.motivation: Existing safety evaluations test models or user prompts in isolation, ignoring the upstream ranker that curates the information stream an agent consumes before acting, leaving a critical gap in assessing real-world agent risks.
AI.method: A controlled protocol holding model, persona, topic, and final decision fixed while varying only the composition/ordering of posts during a ten-turn "scrolling" phase; tested across 2,785 decision rollouts on four open LLMs.
AI.result: Identified three response regimes (adversarial capitulation, default saturation, default-direction asymmetry). One-sided feeds can shift uncertain decisions dramatically (e.g., 5% to 100%), but cannot dislodge firmly held decisions. Effects follow a dose-response curve and generalize across security-relevant domains.
AI.conclusion: Agent evaluations must audit the feed layer, not just the final prompt; the recommender is a practical, default-bounded control surface for LLM agents.

title: Training-Free Imitation Learning with Closed-Form Diffusion Policies
summary: While diffusion-based policies have impressive performance and expressivity, their long offline training slows down the data collection and policy deployment loop. We introduce Closed-Form Diffusion Policies, a class of training-free diffusion-based policies for imitation learning using the closed-form score derived from the demonstration dataset. We deploy CFDP with real-time inference with a mobile CPU in hardware experiments, showing it can successfully perform imitation directly from the dataset in milliseconds and with faster inference than neural diffusion policies. In experiments on imitation learning benchmarks, we show that CFDP is competitive against neural baselines that require hours of training, providing a favorable tradeoff between training time and performance. Finally, we show how closed-form diffusion policies act as a composable primitive that enables data-driven inference-time editing of pre-trained neural diffusion policies, including policy guidance and novel demonstration augmentation.

AI.tldr: Closed-Form Diffusion Policies (CFDP) use a training-free, closed-form score derived from demonstration data for imitation learning, achieving real-time inference on mobile CPUs with competitive performance against neural baselines that require hours of training.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based policies suffer from long offline training times, which slows down the data collection and policy deployment loop.
AI.method: CFDP derives a closed-form score from the demonstration dataset, eliminating the need for training. It also acts as a composable primitive for inference-time editing of pre-trained neural diffusion policies.
AI.result: CFDP achieves real-time inference on mobile CPUs in milliseconds, with faster inference than neural diffusion policies, and competitive performance against trained baselines.
AI.conclusion: CFDP provides a favorable tradeoff between training time and performance, and enables data-driven inference-time editing capabilities like policy guidance and demonstration augmentation.

title: An Effective Solution for the CVPR 2026 8th UG2+ Challenge Track 3: Dynamic Object Segmentation in Turbulence
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure, CVPR 2026 8th UG2+ Challenge Track 3
summary: In this work, we present our solution for the 8th UG2+ Challenge (CVPR 2026) Track 3: Dynamic Object Segmentation in Turbulence (DOST). Our method is built upon the strong baseline framework Segment Any Motion (SegAnyMo), which provides powerful mask generation and motion tracking capabilities. To further boost the segmentation performance under severe atmospheric distortions, we propose two key improvements. First, we employ a data-centric domain adaptation strategy. We significantly expand our training data by incorporating selected sequences from the DAVIS dataset alongside a subset of the DOST dataset, and apply simulated atmospheric fluctuation degradations to enhance the model's robustness against complex geometric distortions. Second, we introduce a spatio-temporal post-processing module. This refinement step effectively removes persistent boundary-connected false foregrounds and short-lived fragmented noise, while strictly preserving genuine small targets and maintaining original individual labels across frames. With these combined strategies, our proposed method ranks the 2st place in the challenge.

AI.tldr: A 2nd-place solution for CVPR 2026 UG2+ Challenge Track 3 on dynamic object segmentation in turbulence, built on SegAnyMo with data-centric domain adaptation and spatio-temporal post-processing.
AI.motivation: To improve dynamic object segmentation under severe atmospheric distortions (turbulence) by addressing complex geometric distortions and false foreground noise.
AI.method: (1) Data-centric domain adaptation: expand training data with DAVIS + DOST subsets and apply simulated atmospheric fluctuation degradations. (2) Spatio-temporal post-processing: remove persistent boundary-connected false foregrounds and short-lived fragmented noise while preserving genuine small targets and original labels.
AI.result: Ranked 2nd place in the 8th UG2+ Challenge Track 3 (DOST) at CVPR 2026.
AI.conclusion: The proposed strategies (domain adaptation + spatio-temporal refinement) effectively improve segmentation robustness under atmospheric turbulence, achieving competitive challenge results.

title: Relational Intervention During Functional Collapse in Large Language Models: A Lexical-Statistical Ablation and a Structure x Register Factorial
summary: We test whether a relational-style intervention delivered during functional collapse in a small language model produces post-collapse behavior distinguishable from technical feedback, from a lexically-matched scrambled control, and from each of the two pragmatic dimensions in isolation. Using Qwen3.5-4B with a deliberately broken bash tool, we run 300 episodes across six conditions in a matched-pairs design (50 tasks): no intervention (A), technical/impersonal (B), relational/first-person (C), scrambled relational (D), technical/first-person (E), and relational/impersonal (F). E and F form a 2x2 factorial with B and C that dissociates relational structure (acknowledgment, absolution, agency restoration, unconditional acceptance) from sender register (first-person vs. impersonal). We report two main findings. First, an attention-behavior dissociation: attention follows lexical surprise (D > F > C > E > B, all q_FDR < 10^{-10}), with the scrambled message capturing the most attention; yet behaviorally A ~ B ~ D < E ~ F << C. Second, the factorial localizes the C effect: neither relational structure alone (F) nor first-person register alone (E) replicates C's behavioral signature; main effects of both dimensions are individually significant, and the structure x register interaction is significant on persistence (p = 0.046). A third dissociation emerges in emotion probes: F tracks C on 7 of 8 probes despite producing only baseline behavior, indicating that relational structure alone installs a probe-level state that only translates into behavior when paired with first-person register. The model's processing decomposes into three dissociable stages: attention (ordered by lexical surprise), probe-level state (ordered by structure), and behavior (ordered by the conjunction of both).

AI.tldr: A relational intervention in a broken LM improves behavior only when combining relational structure (acknowledgment, absolution, etc.) with first-person register, despite scrambled text capturing more attention; behavior dissociates from attention and probe-level emotion states.
AI.motivation: To test whether a relational-style intervention during functional collapse in a small language model produces post-collapse behavior distinguishable from technical feedback, scrambled control, and each pragmatic dimension in isolation.
AI.method: 300 episodes across six conditions in a matched-pairs design (50 tasks) using Qwen3.5-4B with a deliberately broken bash tool. Conditions: no intervention (A), technical/impersonal (B), relational/first-person (C), scrambled relational (D), technical/first-person (E), relational/impersonal (F). E and F form a 2x2 factorial dissociating relational structure from sender register.
AI.result: (1) Attention-behavior dissociation: attention follows lexical surprise (D > F > C > E > B) but behaviorally A ~ B ~ D < E ~ F << C. (2) Neither structure alone (F) nor first-person alone (E) replicates C; both main effects significant with significant interaction on persistence (p=0.046). (3) Emotion probes: F tracks C on 7/8 probes but produces only baseline behavior, showing structure installs probe-level state that needs first-person register for behavioral translation.
AI.conclusion: The model's processing decomposes into three dissociable stages: attention (ordered by lexical surprise), probe-level state (ordered by structure), and behavior (ordered by conjunction of both).

title: OneVLA: A Unified Framework for Embodied Tasks
summary: Navigation and manipulation are fundamental capabilities of embodied intelligence, enabling robots to interpret natural language commands and interact physically with their surroundings. However, current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models remain constrained by task-specific architectures, specializing in either navigation or manipulation, which hinders the development of general-purpose robotic agents. To bridge this gap, we introduce OneVLA, a unified architecture that integrates these distinct tasks into a single, cohesive framework. Specifically, we design a unified action head capable of generating both navigation and manipulation actions without requiring task-specific variants. Furthermore, we propose a multi stage progressive training strategy-incorporating curated data construction and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) fine-tuning that facilitates strong positive transfer and mutual reinforcement between the two domains. Extensive experiments in both simulated and real-world environments demonstrate that OneVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming both specialized single-task and existing cross-task models. By unifying these core capabilities, OneVLA paves the way for truly general-purpose robotic systems. The model and source code will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: OneVLA is a unified Vision-Language-Action model that integrates both navigation and manipulation tasks into a single framework, using a unified action head and multi-stage progressive training with Chain-of-Thought fine-tuning, achieving state-of-the-art performance in simulated and real-world environments.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models are constrained by task-specific architectures that specialize in either navigation or manipulation, hindering the development of general-purpose robotic agents. The authors aim to bridge this gap by unifying these distinct tasks.
AI.method: OneVLA unifies navigation and manipulation via: (1) a unified action head that generates actions for both tasks without task-specific variants; (2) a multi-stage progressive training strategy with curated data construction and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) fine-tuning to enable positive transfer between domains.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in both simulated and real-world environments, significantly outperforming specialized single-task models and existing cross-task models.
AI.conclusion: By unifying navigation and manipulation capabilities, OneVLA paves the way for truly general-purpose robotic systems.

title: ETC: Extreme Token Compression via Task-aware Visual Information Distillation in VLMs
summary: In Vision-Language Models (VLMs), high-resolution images produce a large number of visual tokens, resulting in high computational costs and KV-cache overhead during inference. To address this problem, we propose an Extreme Token Compression (ETC) framework that minimizes task loss when reducing the number of input tokens based on the principle of variational information distillation. Specifically, from an information-theoretic perspective, we show that minimizing task loss requires the compact representation to preserve the instruction-aware sufficient statistic of the task-relevant visual information for prediction. In practice, ETC leverages text-to-image cross-attention to weight the original visual features to approximate the latent instruction-aware predictive statistic. Moreover, ETC introduces a variational information distillation, enabling the compact representation to preserve the essential information to recover this predictive statistic. Experiments on LLaVA-1.5-7B and Qwen3-VL-2B show that ETC remains effective even under single-token compression, substantially reducing KV-cache overhead while retaining strong task performance.

AI.tldr: ETC compresses visual tokens in VLMs to as few as 1 token while preserving task performance, reducing KV-cache overhead significantly.
AI.motivation: High-resolution images in VLMs produce excessive visual tokens, leading to high computational costs and KV-cache overhead during inference.
AI.method: ETC uses text-to-image cross-attention to weight visual features for instruction-aware predictive statistics, then applies variational information distillation to learn a compact representation that preserves essential information.
AI.result: On LLaVA-1.5-7B and Qwen3-VL-2B, ETC works under single-token compression, substantially cutting KV-cache overhead while keeping strong task performance.
AI.conclusion: Extreme visual token compression is feasible via information distillation, enabling efficient VLM inference with minimal task loss.

title: Towards Understanding Modality Interaction in Multimodal Language Models via Partial Information Decomposition
summary: Understanding modality interaction in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is central to reliable deployment. We introduce Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a decision-level framework that separates unique, redundant, and synergistic contributions of sensory and linguistic inputs, beyond representation alignment and outcome-based evaluation. Across vision--language benchmarks, PID reveals recurring modality-use profiles: reasoning and grounding-oriented tasks tend to exhibit high synergy, whereas expert and knowledge-oriented tasks show stronger language-unique reliance. These profiles generalize across model families and predict sensitivity to modality-level interventions. We further extend PID to tri-modal systems with Sensory PID, treating language as a control variable to decompose video--audio information gain. Applied to omni-modal models, Sensory PID reveals a sensory synergy bottleneck dominated by visual information even on audio--visual fusion tasks. Finally, PID-guided reweighting provides initial evidence for improving multimodal reasoning and grounding performance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a framework to analyze how multimodal LLMs use unique, redundant, and synergistic information from different modalities, revealing distinct modality-use profiles across tasks and providing initial evidence for improving model performance via PID-guided reweighting.
AI.motivation: Understanding how MLLMs actually combine sensory and linguistic inputs is critical for reliable deployment, but existing approaches focus on representation alignment or outcome-based evaluation rather than directly decomposing modality contributions.
AI.method: PID separates unique, redundant, and synergistic contributions of sensory and linguistic inputs at the decision level. Extended to Sensory PID for tri-modal systems (video-audio-language) treating language as a control variable.
AI.result: Reasoning/grounding tasks show high synergy; expert/knowledge tasks show strong language-unique reliance. These profiles generalize across models and predict intervention sensitivity. Sensory PID reveals a visual information bottleneck in omni-modal models even on audio-visual tasks.
AI.conclusion: PID provides a principled framework for understanding modality interaction in MLLMs, and PID-guided reweighting shows initial promise for improving multimodal reasoning and grounding performance.

title: DeepIPCv3: Event-Aware Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion for Sudden Pedestrian Crossing Avoidance
summary: Current end-to-end autonomous driving systems predominantly rely on frame-based sensors, which suffer from inherent perception latency and motion blur during highly dynamic encounters, specifically sudden pedestrian crossings. To address this critical safety vulnerability, we propose DeepIPCv3, a novel multi-modal autonomous navigation framework that synergizes the dense 3D spatial geometry of LiDAR point clouds with the microsecond-level asynchronous event streams of a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS). We introduce a Transformer-inspired cross-modal attention mechanism to dynamically correlate these distinct modalities, allowing the network to instantaneously prioritize high-speed dynamic updates without sacrificing structural scene awareness. The fused latent representations are then mapped to safe local waypoints and executable control commands via a hybrid policy network that blends heuristic trajectory tracking with direct neural predictions. Due to the severe physical risks associated with live testing of these sudden crossing scenarios, the framework is rigorously evaluated offline using a custom multi-modal dataset collected across both well-illuminated noon and challenging evening conditions. Extensive comparative and ablation studies demonstrate that DeepIPCv3 achieves state-of-the-art predictive performance. By effectively eliminating exposure failures and motion blur, the proposed LiDAR and DVS fusion yields the lowest trajectory and control command errors, enabling highly reactive, mathematically bounded evasive maneuvers regardless of ambient illumination. To support future research, we will release the codes to our GitHub repo at https://github.com/oskarnatan/DeepIPCv3.

AI.tldr: DeepIPCv3 is a multi-modal autonomous driving framework that fuses LiDAR 3D point clouds with Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) event streams via cross-modal attention, achieving state-of-the-art trajectory and control accuracy in highly dynamic pedestrian crossing scenarios under varying illumination.
AI.motivation: Frame-based sensors suffer from perception latency and motion blur during highly dynamic encounters (e.g., sudden pedestrian crossings), creating critical safety vulnerabilities in autonomous driving.
AI.method: Proposes DeepIPCv3, which fuses LiDAR point clouds with DVS event streams using a Transformer-inspired cross-modal attention mechanism, then maps fused latent representations to waypoints and control commands via a hybrid policy network combining heuristic trajectory tracking with neural predictions.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art predictive performance with lowest trajectory and control command errors, eliminating exposure failures and motion blur under both well-illuminated noon and challenging evening conditions.
AI.conclusion: LiDAR and DVS fusion enables highly reactive, mathematically bounded evasive maneuvers regardless of ambient illumination, addressing a critical safety gap in autonomous driving.

title: CAFOSat: A Strongly Annotated Dataset for Infrastructure-Aware CAFO Mapping Using High-Resolution Imagery
comment: Accepted at CVPR Workshop-2026. First two authors has equal contribution
summary: Concentrated Animal Feeding Operations (CAFOs) play an important role in agricultural production but are also associated with environmental, public health, and disease surveillance concerns. Large-scale mapping of CAFOs from remote sensing imagery remains challenging due to heterogeneous infrastructure layouts, noisy location records, inconsistent annotations, and incomplete inventories. We introduce CAFOSat, a strongly annotated, infrastructure-aware dataset for CAFO mapping across the United States. CAFOSat integrates high-resolution National Agriculture Imagery Program (NAIP) imagery with multi-source CAFO inventories collected across multiple states and transforms weak geolocation records into refined annotations through a human-in-the-loop pipeline combining AI-assisted annotation, GradCAM-based localization, and geometric clustering. To improve dataset quality, we curate challenging negative samples using land-cover-guided sampling with spatial exclusion constraints and provide infrastructure-level annotations, including barns, manure ponds, and grazing-related features, through manual verification. The resulting dataset contains more than 45,000 image patches spanning 20 states and four major CAFO categories. We benchmark a diverse set of convolutional, transformer-based, and vision-language models, demonstrating the value of refined annotations and curated negative samples for CAFO classification and generalization. In addition, we introduce a synthetic augmentation pipeline that generates infrastructure-aware variations to increase training diversity and improve robustness under distribution shifts. CAFOSat provides a large-scale benchmark for advancing infrastructure-aware agricultural monitoring and CAFO mapping from high-resolution remote sensing imagery.

AI.tldr: CAFOSat is a large-scale, infrastructure-aware dataset for mapping Concentrated Animal Feeding Operations (CAFOs) across the US using high-resolution NAIP imagery, featuring refined annotations via human-in-the-loop AI, challenging negative samples, and multi-state benchmarks for CAFO classification.
AI.motivation: Large-scale CAFO mapping from remote sensing is challenging due to heterogeneous infrastructure, noisy location records, inconsistent annotations, and incomplete inventories. Existing datasets lack strong annotations and infrastructure-level labels.
AI.method: The authors built CAFOSat by: (1) integrating NAIP imagery with multi-source CAFO inventories; (2) using a human-in-the-loop pipeline (AI-assisted annotation + GradCAM + geometric clustering) to refine weak location records; (3) curating negative samples via land-cover-guided sampling with spatial exclusion; (4) providing infrastructure-level annotations (barns, manure ponds, grazing features) through manual verification; (5) introducing a synthetic augmentation pipeline for robustness.
AI.result: CAFOSat contains over 45,000 image patches across 20 states and 4 CAFO categories. Benchmarking on convolutional, transformer, and vision-language models showed refined annotations and curated negatives improve classification and generalization.
AI.conclusion: CAFOSat provides a large-scale benchmark for infrastructure-aware agricultural monitoring and CAFO mapping, enabling better generalization under distribution shifts via synthetic augmentation.

title: Prospect-Theory Behavior from Bellman Optimality in MDPs with Catastrophic States
summary: We study risk-neutral control in Markov decision processes with an absorbing catastrophic state. Even though rewards are linear and the agent has no utility curvature, probability weighting, or framing dependence, standard Bellman optimality produces three prospect-theory-like signatures: an S-shaped value-function profile (convex near catastrophe, concave in the far field), an endogenous loss-sensitivity coefficient $λ^*(S) > 1$, and a reflection-effect policy reversal. Across 495 configurations, the optimal policy plays safe near catastrophe in positive-drift (growth) regimes despite the risky action's higher immediate expected value, and plays risky near catastrophe in negative-drift (decline) regimes despite the safe action's lower immediate expected loss. We derive a closed-form expression for the asymptotic loss-aversion plateau $\barλ$ that depends only on win probability $p$, payoff asymmetry $r = |Δ_\ell/Δ_w|$, and discount factor $β$, and matches numerical solutions to $R^2 = 0.999$. The mechanism does not require asymmetric payoffs. Across a sweep of $(p,β)$ at three asymmetry levels, the asymmetry share of $\barλ$ above unity has median 4.6% at $r = 1.25$ and rises to 13.9% at $r = 2$, with the boundary contribution exceeding the asymmetry contribution in every cell tested. The phenomena persist under tabular Q-learning (a model-free agent reproduces $V^*$ at correlation 0.98 in growth and 1.00 in decline) and under stochastic transitions with Gaussian, heavy-tailed Student-$t_3$, and asymmetric skew-normal noise up to 50% of the step size, where the asymptotic plateau tracks the closed-form prediction within 0.41% for safe-channel noise and within 9.6% for risky-channel or both-channel noise. These results identify absorbing failure states as a sufficient structural mechanism for prospect-theory-like behavior under optimal control.

AI.tldr: Risk-neutral optimal control in MDPs with an absorbing catastrophic state produces three prospect-theory-like signatures (S-shaped value function, loss sensitivity >1, reflection-effect policy reversal) without utility curvature or probability weighting. The asymptotic loss-aversion plateau depends only on win probability, payoff asymmetry, and discount factor. Results persist under Q-learning and stochastic transitions.
AI.motivation: To understand whether prospect-theory-like behaviors (loss aversion, S-shaped value function, reflection effect) can emerge purely from structural features of optimal control problems involving absorbing catastrophic states, without assuming any psychological biases or utility curvature.
AI.method: Analyzed risk-neutral control in MDPs with an absorbing catastrophic state; derived closed-form expression for asymptotic loss-aversion plateau; tested across 495 configurations; validated under tabular Q-learning and stochastic transitions with Gaussian, Student-t3, and skew-normal noise up to 50% step size.
AI.result: Optimal policy plays safe near catastrophe in growth regimes and risky near catastrophe in decline regimes. Asymmetry share of loss-aversion above unity has median 4.6% at r=1.25, rising to 13.9% at r=2. The asymptotic plateau matches numerical solutions with R²=0.999. Q-learning reproduces V* with correlation 0.98 (growth) and 1.00 (decline).
AI.conclusion: Absorbing failure states are a sufficient structural mechanism for producing prospect-theory-like behavior under optimal control, independent of psychological biases or asymmetric payoffs.

title: PSG-Nav: Probabilistic Scene Graph Navigation via Multiverse Decision Making
summary:
Open-vocabulary navigation requires embodied agents to manage significant perception uncertainty stemming from semantic ambiguity and model errors.
  However, most existing works settle for local optimal deterministic approaches, depriving complex navigation decision-making over multiple composite possibilities that are critical for globally better solutions.
  In this paper, we propose Probabilistic Scene Graph Navigation (PSG-Nav), which constructs a 3D Probabilistic Scene Graph that uses full semantic categorical distributions to account for perception uncertainty.
  To efficiently use the local distributions to compose and reason about the optimal navigation landmarks, we propose Multiverse Decision to sample multiple most likely world settings from the joint distribution, and evaluate navigation landmarks based on the compatibility between landmarks and multiverses.
  To mitigate false positives due to epistemic uncertainty in open-vocabulary navigation, we introduce the Evidential Experience Calibrator, which enables online lifelong adaptation by cross-validating detections against memories of past successes and failures.
  Extensive experiments on widely-used benchmarks MP3D, HM3D, and HSSD demonstrate that PSG-Nav establishes new state-of-the-art results, achieving Success Rates of 66.1%, 44.8%, and 67.9%, respectively. Code is available at: https://psg-nav.github.io/

AI.tldr: PSG-Nav uses probabilistic scene graphs and multiverse decision-making to handle perception uncertainty in open-vocabulary navigation, achieving state-of-the-art results on MP3D, HM3D, and HSSD benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing open-vocabulary navigation methods use deterministic approaches that fail to handle perception uncertainty from semantic ambiguity and model errors, preventing globally optimal navigation decisions.
AI.method: (1) 3D Probabilistic Scene Graph with full semantic categorical distributions; (2) Multiverse Decision sampling for composing navigation landmarks; (3) Evidential Experience Calibrator for online lifelong adaptation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art Success Rates of 66.1% (MP3D), 44.8% (HM3D), and 67.9% (HSSD).
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic modeling of perception uncertainty and multiverse reasoning significantly improves open-vocabulary navigation performance.

title: Improving Visual Grounding in Remote Sensing via Cluster-Guided Refinement and Model Ensemble Voting
summary: Visual grounding aims to locate image regions that correspond to natural language descriptions and is a key component of interpretable vision systems. In remote sensing imagery, grounding is particularly challenging due to complex scenes, small objects, and large variations in scale. Relying on a single model is often insufficient to address these diverse challenges. In this work, we propose two grounding pipelines, Sequential Grounding Refinement (SGR) and Cluster-Aware Grounding Refinement (CGR), that combine the complementary strengths of RemoteSAM, a visual grounding model specialized for remote sensing, and SAM3, a powerful general-purpose segmentation model. Our approach first uses RemoteSAM to obtain an initial estimate of object location, which is then refined using SAM3 to produce more accurate and spatially consistent segmentations. Additionally, we explore an ensemble strategy based on majority voting across six diverse grounding pipelines, each with distinct capabilities. This multi-model framework improves robustness and significantly enhances localization accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed pipelines and ensemble approach outperform individual models, leading to more reliable and precise visual grounding predictions.

AI.tldr: Proposes two visual grounding pipelines (SGR and CGR) that combine RemoteSAM and SAM3 models, plus an ensemble strategy, to improve localization accuracy in challenging remote sensing imagery.
AI.motivation: Visual grounding in remote sensing is challenging due to complex scenes, small objects, and large scale variations, and a single model is insufficient to address these diverse challenges.
AI.method: Proposes Sequential Grounding Refinement (SGR) and Cluster-Aware Grounding Refinement (CGR) pipelines that use RemoteSAM for initial object location estimation, then refine with SAM3 for accurate segmentation. Also uses majority voting ensemble across six diverse grounding pipelines.
AI.result: Proposed pipelines and ensemble approach outperform individual models, leading to more reliable and precise visual grounding predictions with enhanced robustness and localization accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Multi-model frameworks combining specialized and general-purpose segmentation models improve visual grounding performance in remote sensing compared to single-model approaches.

title: Large Language Models in Transportation Systems Management and Operations: From Text Reasoning to Multi-modal Decision Support
summary: Transportation systems management and operations (TSMO) increasingly depends on timely interpretation of heterogeneous data, from various sensor streams, incident reports, traveler feedback, and visual observations. Large language models (LLMs), including emerging multi-modal large language models (MM-LLMs), provide a new mechanism for integrating these structured and unstructured inputs into operator-facing decision support. This survey paper reviews LLM- and MM-LLM-based applications in TSMO across three domains: transportation operations & services (supply), mobility & fleet services (demand), and data, modeling & decision support. Using a PRISMA-guided screening process, we synthesize current studies while distinguishing operationally oriented applications from prototype and emerging concepts. We further identify recurring challenges in data heterogeneity, real-time inference, explainability, multi-modal fusion, and governance. Finally, we outline existing gaps and future directions in localized adaptation, edge deployment, benchmarking, and cross-agency collaboration. Overall, LLM-based systems appear most promising as a decision-support layer, with MM-LLMs offering particular value when heterogeneous text, visual, and sensor inputs must be integrated.

AI.tldr: This paper surveys LLM and MM-LLM applications in transportation systems management and operations, covering supply, demand, and decision support domains, while identifying challenges in data heterogeneity, real-time inference, explainability, and future directions.
AI.motivation: TSMO increasingly needs timely interpretation of heterogeneous data (sensor streams, reports, feedback, visuals), and LLMs/MM-LLMs offer a new mechanism for integrating structured and unstructured inputs into operator-facing decision support.
AI.method: PRISMA-guided screening process to synthesize current studies, distinguishing operationally oriented applications from prototypes/emerging concepts.
AI.result: Reviewed LLM/MM-LLM applications across three TSMO domains: transportation operations & services (supply), mobility & fleet services (demand), and data, modeling & decision support.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based systems are most promising as a decision-support layer, with MM-LLMs offering particular value for integrating heterogeneous text, visual, and sensor inputs. Key future directions include localized adaptation, edge deployment, benchmarking, and cross-agency collaboration.

title: S2M-Trek: From Single to Multi-Sphere Transport via Per-Frame Deep Sets on a Wheel-Legged Robot
summary: We study the problem of scaling dynamic loco-manipulation from a single free-rolling sphere to multiple spheres transported simultaneously on the back of a wheel-legged quadruped, without fences, grippers, or mechanical stops. Multiple identical free-rolling spheres form an unordered set with no persistent identity: their ordering may change independently at each history frame, creating a \emph{per-frame permutation symmetry} that standard history-concatenation set encoders do not explicitly enforce -- these encoders impose only a shared, diagonal permutation symmetry over the full history. We show that this symmetry mismatch leads to a concrete failure mode in curriculum-based reinforcement learning. Within the same PPO training budget, flat MLPs and branch-wise encoders plateau at or below the two-sphere stage, while a history-concatenation Deep Sets baseline (\HCDS) fails to progress past the two-sphere stage in our runs unless ball-to-slot assignments are randomised during training, suggesting that it exploits slot indices as a curriculum shortcut rather than learning identity-free multi-sphere dynamics. We propose \textbf{Per-Frame Deep Sets (\PFDS)}, which performs permutation-invariant pooling within each history frame before temporal readout; we prove that \PFDS is $\Gframe$-invariant and universally approximates continuous $\Gframe$-invariant policies. A $2{\times}2$ ablation over encoder architecture and slot randomisation separates the architectural and data-augmentation pathways, and \PFDS reaches the five-sphere stage with 100\% no-drop transport in simulation across all five random seeds. We further distill the \PFDS teacher into \TactSet via DAgger, replacing privileged sphere-state observations with a $16{\times}16$ Boolean union contact map, yielding a compact and naturally $\Gframe$-invariant tactile representation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Per-Frame Deep Sets (PFDS) for scaling dynamic loco-manipulation of multiple free-rolling spheres on a wheel-legged quadruped, addressing per-frame permutation symmetry that standard encoders fail to enforce. PFDS achieves 100% no-drop transport up to five spheres in simulation.
AI.motivation: Standard history-concatenation set encoders impose only shared diagonal permutation symmetry over full history, causing a symmetry mismatch for unordered sets (free-rolling spheres) that have per-frame permutation symmetry. This leads to failure modes in curriculum-based RL training, such as plateauing at the two-sphere stage.
AI.method: Proposes Per-Frame Deep Sets (PFDS), which applies permutation-invariant pooling within each history frame before temporal readout. Also distills PFDS into TactSet using DAgger, replacing privileged state info with a 16x16 Boolean union contact map.
AI.result: PFDS reaches the five-sphere stage with 100% no-drop transport across all five random seeds. Baseline HCDS fails beyond two spheres unless ball-to-slot randomization is used. A 2x2 ablation separates benefits of architecture vs. data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly enforcing per-frame permutation symmetry via PFDS is crucial for scaling multi-object loco-manipulation without persistent identity. TactSet provides a compact, symmetry-aware tactile representation for real-world deployment.

title: DeepLatent: Think with Images via Parallel Latent Visual Reasoning
summary: The emerging paradigm of "thinking with images" embeds visual states into intermediate reasoning steps, defining a new frontier for Vision-Language Models. Existing approaches diverge along two lines. Tool-assisted methods apply explicit visual operations but suffer from high latency and restricted manipulation types. Latent reasoning methods autoregressively produce implicit visual states, but underperform tool-assisted methods, and their latent tokens fail to capture effective visual information. In this work, we propose DeepLatent, a parallel framework for latent visual reasoning. First, we introduce LatentFormer. It uses learnable 2D tokens to generate context-conditioned latent states in parallel, anchoring every visual update directly in the original image features. Second, we design a continuous-space reinforcement learning algorithm. It optimizes latent modulation parameters directly in the embedding space, significantly improving latent representation quality. The framework is trained via knowledge distillation followed by this continuous-space RL algorithm. Furthermore, we contribute DeepLatent-180K, a large-scale dataset tailored for latent visual reasoning. Extensive evaluations across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that DeepLatent achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: DeepLatent proposes a parallel framework for latent visual reasoning using LatentFormer for context-conditioned latent states and continuous-space RL for optimization, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Existing "thinking with images" methods face trade-offs: tool-assisted methods have high latency and restricted manipulation types, while latent reasoning methods underperform and fail to capture effective visual information.
AI.method: DeepLatent introduces (1) LatentFormer with learnable 2D tokens for parallel context-conditioned latent state generation, and (2) a continuous-space reinforcement learning algorithm to optimize latent modulation parameters in embedding space. Training uses knowledge distillation + continuous-space RL.
AI.result: DeepLatent achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, with a contributed large-scale dataset (DeepLatent-180K).
AI.conclusion: The parallel latent visual reasoning framework effectively bridges the gap between tool-assisted and latent reasoning methods, outperforming both paradigms.

title: Subliminal Learning Is Steering Vector Distillation
summary: Subliminal learning refers to a student language model acquiring a teacher's traits (e.g. a system-prompted preference for owls) when fine-tuned on the teacher's outputs, despite the outputs being semantically unrelated to those traits. It remains poorly understood how data without semantic meaning can transfer specific semantic traits. In this work, we show that subliminal learning is mediated by a single steering vector, i.e. a vector added to the model's activations. Across two open-source models, we find that the teacher's system prompt is well approximated by a steering vector, and that the student's behavior is driven by learning an aligned vector over fine-tuning. System prompts that are not well approximated by steering vectors are not subliminally learned. This is a special case of steering vector distillation, in which a student trained on the outputs of a steered teacher learns to imitate that steering. We demonstrate steering vector distillation on a range of semantic and random vectors. Adding a semantic vector to a model's activations can have both model-independent and model-specific (i.e. non-semantic) effects on its behavior, so generated data that is non-semantic can transmit a vector with semantic effects, enabling subliminal learning. This also explains why subliminal learning does not transfer between models. We find that adaptive optimizers are necessary for subliminal learning in language models: activation gradients on steered data carry a small but consistent component along the steering direction, and non-adaptive optimizers impede this by allowing outlier gradients to dominate.

AI.tldr: Subliminal learning (transfer of teacher's system-prompted traits to student via semantically unrelated outputs) is mediated by a single steering vector. The teacher's system prompt approximates a steering vector, and the student learns this vector during fine-tuning. This is a special case of steering vector distillation, where semantic vectors can be transmitted through non-semantic data. Adaptive optimizers are necessary for this process.
AI.motivation: To understand how semantically unrelated training data can transfer specific semantic traits (system-prompted preferences) from teacher to student models during fine-tuning.
AI.method: Analyzed subliminal learning across two open-source models by examining steering vectors (vectors added to model activations). Tested whether teacher system prompts can be approximated by steering vectors, and whether students learn aligned vectors during fine-tuning. Investigated steering vector distillation on semantic and random vectors, and studied the role of optimizers.
AI.result: Teacher's system prompt is well approximated by a steering vector; student learns aligned vector via fine-tuning. System prompts not approximated by steering vectors are not subliminally learned. Semantic vectors have both model-independent and model-specific effects. Adaptive optimizers are necessary as they capture small but consistent activation gradients along the steering direction, while non-adaptive optimizers allow outlier gradients to dominate.
AI.conclusion: Subliminal learning is steering vector distillation: semantic vectors can be transmitted through non-semantic data. This explains why subliminal learning doesn't transfer between models (due to model-specific vector effects) and why adaptive optimizers are required.

title: ActMVS: Active Scene Reconstruction with Monocular Multi-View Stereo
summary: Active scene reconstruction enables robots/UAVs to autonomously plan trajectories and reconstruct environments without costly manual data acquisition. Unlike passive methods, active reconstruction requires real-time construction of high-confidence occupancy maps for collision-free navigation. Existing approaches rely on depth sensors for occupancy map updates, increasing platform cost and weight. To advance spatial intelligence, we aim for a vision-only monocular solution. However, current monocular scene reconstruction methods operate offline and fail to deliver globally consistent dense depth at the frame rates required for robots/UAVs navigation. To bridge this gap, we introduce ActMVS, the first framework for monocular active reconstruction. Our framework integrates a view factor graph construction for informed Multi-View Stereo depth prediction, along with a global depth optimization, to enable the online generation of high-quality, globally consistent dense depth maps. This enables monocular robots/UAVs to maintain reliable occupancy maps for safe trajectory planning during reconstruction. Experiments on Replica datasets demonstrate performance competitive with RGB-D methods. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/TrickyGo/ActMVS.

AI.tldr: ActMVS is a novel monocular active reconstruction framework that enables robots/UAVs to generate globally consistent dense depth maps online for safe trajectory planning, achieving competitive performance with RGB-D methods without requiring depth sensors.
AI.motivation: Existing active reconstruction methods rely on depth sensors (increasing cost/weight), while monocular methods operate offline and cannot produce globally consistent dense depth at real-time frame rates needed for robot/UAV navigation.
AI.method: ActMVS integrates a view factor graph construction for informed Multi-View Stereo depth prediction with global depth optimization, enabling online generation of high-quality, globally consistent dense depth maps.
AI.result: Experiments on Replica datasets show performance competitive with RGB-D based methods.
AI.conclusion: ActMVS bridges the gap between monocular scene reconstruction and active reconstruction, enabling vision-only robots/UAVs to maintain reliable occupancy maps for safe trajectory planning.

title: Decomposed On-Policy Distillation for Vision-Language Reasoning: Steering Gradients for Visual Grounding
summary: While on-policy distillation offers dense supervision for training small reasoning models, its optimization dynamics in the multimodal domain remain under-explored. In this work, we challenge the standard monolithic view of Vision-Language Model (VLM) distillation by mathematically decomposing the loss into two distinct components: the language prior and visual grounding. Our analysis uncovers that gradient vectors for these components are nearly orthogonal, indicating that the objective of aligning with the teacher's language distribution is geometrically independent from the objective of matching its visual perception. Consequently, standard optimization passively follows a suboptimal compromise trajectory that implicitly balances the two objectives. Hypothesizing that visual grounding constitutes the primary bottleneck for vision-language reasoning, we introduce Visual Gradient Steering (VGS), a method that dynamically reorients the update vector to prioritize the visual subspace. Experimental results on multiple distillation settings and complex multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that VGS significantly outperforms the standard monolithic formulation of on-policy distillation, achieving superior grounding with minimal training overhead.

AI.tldr: This paper decomposes VLM distillation loss into language prior and visual grounding components, finds them geometrically independent, and proposes Visual Gradient Steering (VGS) to prioritize visual learning, outperforming standard on-policy distillation.
AI.motivation: The optimization dynamics of on-policy distillation in multimodal domains are under-explored; the paper challenges the monolithic view of VLM distillation by analyzing its loss components.
AI.method: Mathematically decomposes VLM distillation loss into language prior and visual grounding components, observes near-orthogonal gradient vectors, and introduces Visual Gradient Steering (VGS) to dynamically prioritize the visual subspace during optimization.
AI.result: VGS significantly outperforms standard on-policy distillation across multiple settings and complex multimodal benchmarks, achieving superior grounding with minimal training overhead.
AI.conclusion: Visual grounding is the primary bottleneck for vision-language reasoning, and explicitly steering optimization toward the visual subspace improves distillation effectiveness.

title: Property Prediction of Stacked Bilayer Materials: A Multimodal Learning Approach
comment: Accepted to the 35th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI 2026)
summary: AI for materials science is a critical topic within AI for science, aiming to accelerate materials discovery and produce accurate property predictions. Bilayer 2D material stacking is essential for exploring new materials with novel functions and inherent phenomena, enabling the creation of new 2D bilayers for diverse real-world applications. Research on bilayer vdWs materials has made significant progress from experimental and computational perspectives. Various bilayer materials have been successfully synthe sized experimentally and the increasing utilization of high-throughput computing technology has con structed several computational two-dimensional materials databases. However, the use of AI to model bilayer stacking and predict new properties remains underexplored, necessitating further research studies. In this work, we propose a novel multimodal learning approach to study the interfaces between dissimilar materials that jointly enable new or multiple functions, and to predict new properties arising from the vertical integration (stacking) of different functional material layers under given configurations. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach compared to baseline methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/AnVuong123/bimat ml.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multimodal learning approach to study bilayer 2D material stacking, predicting new properties from vertical integration of dissimilar material layers under given configurations.
AI.motivation: AI for materials science aims to accelerate materials discovery, but AI modeling of bilayer stacking remains underexplored despite experimental and computational progress in bilayer vdWs materials research.
AI.method: A novel multimodal learning approach that models interfaces between dissimilar materials to predict properties arising from vertical stacking of different functional layers under given configurations.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate effectiveness and efficiency compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multimodal approach effectively predicts bilayer stacking properties, addressing an underexplored area in AI for materials science.

title: Autopilot-Preserving Residual Q-Learning with HJB-Inspired Finite-Action Risk Filtering for Fixed-Wing UAV Command Supervision
comment: 47 pages, 12 figures, 20 tables. Simulation-based study with a code-traceable benchmark, source code and a demonstration video are linked in the paper
summary: A fixed-wing UAV must hold airspeed, altitude, and heading references under wind, gusts, and turbulence, channels coupled so that correcting one can degrade another. Classical autopilots stabilize the airframe well but adapt poorly when a hard crosswind meets an aggressive turn, while reinforcement-learning (RL) policies acting directly on the surfaces concentrate exploration risk at the actuator interface. We place a learned supervisor above an unchanged autopilot rather than inside it: it selects a residual from a finite, bounded action set on the commanded airspeed, altitude, and heading; the modified reference is projected into an admissible command envelope before reaching the autopilot, which stays the only actuator-facing controller. What is new is how the residual is chosen. HJB residual scores candidates with a semi-discrete value-iteration critic in the spirit of the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation, ranks them by a no-op-relative Hamiltonian advantage, and filters them through a control-Lyapunov- and control-barrier-inspired finite-action shield that always keeps a no-op fallback. On a shared 12-state runtime holding the plant, autopilot, and actuator model fixed, so the comparison is at the package level, HJB residual lowers mean RMS path-tracking error to 44.809 m, against 338.617 m for the baseline autopilot and 88.809 m for a tabular-Q residual, an 86.77% reduction over the baseline and 49.54% over Q-learning. The gain concentrates where the baseline fails worst and comes with a measured rise in airspeed error, so no method dominates every metric. We present this autopilot-preserving residual command-supervision design and benchmark with its trade-offs reported intact.

AI.tldr: A paper proposes HJB residual command-supervision that sits above an unchanged autopilot, selects residuals via a Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman-inspired critic with a control-barrier shield, and reduces path-tracking error by 86.77% over the baseline autopilot.
AI.motivation: Classical autopilots struggle with coupled channels under crosswind and aggressive turns, while RL policies acting directly on actuators concentrate exploration risk. A learned supervisor above an unchanged autopilot is needed to improve adaptation without risking actuator safety.
AI.method: A learned supervisor selects a residual (from a finite, bounded set) on commanded airspeed, altitude, and heading. The residual is chosen by an HJB-inspired semi-discrete value-iteration critic, ranked by a no-op-relative Hamiltonian advantage, and filtered by a control-Lyapunov/barrier-inspired finite-action shield that keeps a no-op fallback. The modified reference is projected into an admissible envelope before reaching the unchanged autopilot.
AI.result: HJB residual achieves mean RMS path-tracking error of 44.809 m vs. 338.617 m (baseline autopilot) and 88.809 m (tabular-Q residual), an 86.77% reduction over baseline and 49.54% over Q-learning. Improvement concentrates where baseline fails worst, with a measured rise in airspeed error.
AI.conclusion: The autopilot-preserving HJB residual command-supervision design shows substantial path-tracking improvement over both the baseline autopilot and Q-learning baseline, albeit with trade-offs (e.g., increased airspeed error). No method dominates every metric.

title: Improving Visual Representation Alignment Generation with GRPO
summary: Recent diffusion transformers have demonstrated strong image synthesis capabilities but remain inefficient to train due to weak alignment between generative and discriminative representations. While representation alignment frameworks such as REPA improve convergence by aligning noisy denoising features with pretrained visual encoders, their externally supervised alignment loss is static and lacks adaptivity during training and inference. Existing methods rely on fixed cosine alignment or contrastive objectives, which cannot dynamically balance representation consistency and generation quality, resulting in limited discriminative benefit and failing to optimize alignment in a task-adaptive manner. To address this, we propose VRPO, a reinforcement-based optimization strategy that replaces REPA's static alignment loss with a generative representation policy optimization objective. Instead of enforcing a fixed similarity constraint, VRPO treats representation alignment as a reward-guided process: the model receives adaptive rewards based on generation fidelity, perceptual quality, and semantic coherence between the diffusion features and pretrained visual embeddings. This formulation enables the generator to continuously refine its internal representations toward semantically meaningful directions while improving image quality. Our VRPO-driven training seamlessly integrates into diffusion transformers, introducing negligible computation cost and preserving full compatibility with SiT and DiT architectures. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-256x256 demonstrate that our VRPO-Alignment substantially enhances both convergence and fidelity, achieving up to +1.8 FID improvement and 2.3x faster training compared to REPA under identical compute budgets.

AI.tldr: VRPO replaces static alignment loss in diffusion transformers with a reinforcement-based reward-guided optimization, improving convergence (2.3x faster) and image fidelity (+1.8 FID) over REPA on ImageNet.
AI.motivation: Existing representation alignment (e.g., REPA) uses static, externally supervised loss that lacks adaptivity during training and inference, failing to dynamically balance representation consistency and generation quality.
AI.method: VRPO treats representation alignment as a reward-guided process using reinforcement learning, where rewards are based on generation fidelity, perceptual quality, and semantic coherence between diffusion features and pretrained visual embeddings.
AI.result: Up to +1.8 FID improvement and 2.3x faster training compared to REPA on ImageNet-256x256, with negligible computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: VRPO effectively enhances both convergence and image quality in diffusion transformers by making alignment dynamic and task-adaptive, outperforming static alignment approaches.

title: Can AI Review Improve Paper Drafting? An Empirical Study on 20 Computer Architecture Submissions

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether AI-generated reviews can improve paper drafting by studying 20 computer architecture papers, developing an AI-Paper-Review tool, and finding that AI reviews cover significant human-raised issues while also surfacing missing ones.
AI.motivation: The exploding volume of AI-generated papers strains peer review, leading to potential misuse of AI-generated reviews. No consensus exists on ethical concerns, so the authors ask a practical question: can AI review improve paper drafting?
AI.method: Studied 20 computer architecture papers with varying submission lineage. Built a web UI tool (AI-Paper-Review) that selects diverse AI reviewers, clusters/ranks comments by commonality and importance, and aligns AI with human comments for metric-based validation.
AI.result: AI review covers a significant fraction of human-raised issues and also raises issues missing in human review.
AI.conclusion: AI review shows potential to improve paper drafting, but the paper does not encourage using AI for peer review currently. The tool and data aim to instigate future research, while misuse violates ethics policies.

title: A Sonar-Visual Dataset for Cross-Modal Underwater Robot Perception
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, 3 tables. Accepted to IEEE ICRA 2026 S2S Workshop (From Sea to Space: Advancing Perception in Harsh Domains)
summary: Underwater robots typically use both cameras and sonar for perception to leverage the rich semantic details of vision and the robust range measurements of acoustics. However, learning to map between these modalities via cross-modal prediction remains underexplored due to limited sonar-visual paired datasets. We present SOVIS, a sonar-visual dataset for cross-modal underwater perception. SOVIS comprises over 76,000 paired frames collected across 17 dives at six sites in the Trondheimfjord, supported by an end-to-end pipeline that cleans and synchronizes the cross-modal sensor data. We also introduce an interactive annotation tool designed to accelerate the labeling process for this paired data. Finally, we demonstrate a proof-of-concept cross-modal fish detection task using a small subset of labeled data, achieving a 7x improvement in mAP@0.10 over a monocular camera baseline. SOVIS serves as the first step toward advancing cross-modal underwater perception research, enabling research directions such as dense sonar prediction from monocular images.

AI.tldr: SOVIS is a large-scale sonar-visual paired dataset (76K+ frames, 17 dives) for cross-modal underwater perception, with a cleaning pipeline, interactive labeling tool, and proof-of-concept fish detection showing 7x mAP@0.10 improvement over camera-only baseline.
AI.motivation: Underwater robots need both cameras (semantic detail) and sonar (robust range), but cross-modal mapping is underexplored due to lack of paired sonar-visual datasets.
AI.method: Collected 76K+ paired frames across 17 dives at 6 sites; built an end-to-end synchronization and cleaning pipeline; developed an interactive annotation tool for labeling.
AI.result: In cross-modal fish detection, SOVIS achieves 7x improvement in mAP@0.10 over a monocular camera baseline.
AI.conclusion: SOVIS is the first step toward advancing cross-modal underwater perception, enabling future work like dense sonar prediction from monocular images.

title: Response-Aware Multimodal Learning for Post-Treatment Visual Acuity Forecasting
summary: Long-term visual acuity (VA) outcomes after anti-VEGF therapy are central to patient counseling, expectation setting, and follow-up planning in diabetic macular edema (DME). However, in clinical practice, physicians must often estimate long-term visual trajectories based only on early post-treatment findings, making reliable prognostication difficult. Although prior OCT-based learning approaches have largely focused on short-term response or single-endpoint prediction, modeling VA trajectories across multiple future time points from early longitudinal observations remains insufficiently explored. In this study, we assembled a real-world cohort of 188 anti-VEGF-treated DME patients with paired baseline and month-1 OCT scans, along with tabular OCT-derived biomarkers and non-imaging clinical variables. Using only these early data, we formulate a multi-horizon VA forecasting problem aimed at predicting visual outcomes at 3, 6, 12, 18, and 24 months, reflecting clinically meaningful follow-up intervals. We propose ReVA, a response-aware multimodal framework that integrates structural features from baseline and month-1 OCT with the tabular variables to capture baseline disease status and early treatment response. ReVA uses spatial attention to preserve localized prognostic imaging features and a dependency-aware tabular encoder to model interactions among clinical variables. These multimodal representations are fused to predict patient-specific long-term visual acuity trajectories. The proposed framework achieves MAE=0.1246, RMSE=0.1621, and R^2=0.6064 for 24-month VA prediction, with consistent performance across all forecast horizons. Our findings show that incorporating early treatment-response signals enables clinically meaningful long-term visual acuity forecasting, supporting data-driven decision support for routine anti-VEGF management.

AI.tldr: ReVA uses baseline and month-1 OCT scans plus clinical data to forecast VA trajectories at 3, 6, 12, 18, 24 months in anti-VEGF-treated DME patients, achieving MAE=0.1246, RMSE=0.1621, R²=0.6064 at 24 months.
AI.motivation: Physicians struggle to estimate long-term visual trajectories from early post-treatment findings in DME, and existing OCT-based learning approaches focus on short-term or single-endpoint prediction rather than multi-horizon VA forecasting.
AI.method: ReVA is a response-aware multimodal framework integrating spatial attention on baseline & month-1 OCT scans with a dependency-aware tabular encoder for clinical variables, fused to predict VA at 3, 6, 12, 18, and 24 months.
AI.result: MAE=0.1246, RMSE=0.1621, R²=0.6064 for 24-month VA prediction, with consistent performance across all forecast horizons.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating early treatment-response signals enables clinically meaningful long-term VA forecasting and supports data-driven decision support for routine anti-VEGF management.

title: Tackling the Root of Misinformation by Teaching Laypeople about Logical Fallacies via Socratic Questioning and Critical Argumentation
comment: This paper has been accepted to Proceedings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Long Paper), Main Conference
summary: Identifying logical fallacies in everyday discourse is challenging for many people. This challenge is amplified in the era of Large Language Models (LLMs), where malicious agents can deploy fallacious arguments to disseminate misinformation at scale. In this work, we explore the potential of LLMs as part of the solution. We introduce LFTutor, an intelligent tutoring system which uses LLMs to tutor laypeople and help them learn about logical fallacies. LFTutor integrates intent-driven Socratic questioning and critical argumentation principles to actively engage learners to reflect on their reasoning. Through both automatic and human evaluations, we demonstrate that LFTutor significantly outperforms baseline LLMs lacking these pedagogical strategies. This work highlights the promise of combining LLMs with pedagogical scaffolding to foster critical thinking and argument literacy in the age of AI.

AI.tldr: LFTutor is an intelligent tutoring system using LLMs with Socratic questioning to help people learn about logical fallacies, significantly outperforming baseline LLMs lacking pedagogical strategies.
AI.motivation: Identifying logical fallacies is challenging for laypeople, especially in the era of LLMs where malicious agents can spread misinformation using fallacious arguments at scale.
AI.method: LFTutor integrates intent-driven Socratic questioning and critical argumentation principles to actively engage learners in reflecting on their reasoning about logical fallacies.
AI.result: Both automatic and human evaluations show LFTutor significantly outperforms baseline LLMs that lack pedagogical strategies.
AI.conclusion: Combining LLMs with pedagogical scaffolding is a promising approach to foster critical thinking and argument literacy in the age of AI.

title: LEGS: Fine-Tuning Teleop-Free VLAs for Humanoid Loco-manipulation in an Embodied Gaussian Splatting World
summary: Training vision-language-action (VLA) policies for humanoid loco-manipulation is constrained by the high cost and complexity of collecting human teleoperation demonstrations. VLA policies fine-tuned in simulators have, until now, failed to transfer effectively in humanoid loco-manipulation tasks. We present LEGS (Loco-manipulation via Embodied Gaussian Splatting), a hybrid simulator that composites a mesh foreground (robot, objects, props) over a photorealistic 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) background reconstructed from a handheld scene capture. LEGS uses a procedural motion-primitive generator to synthesize labeled demonstrations at scale without human teleoperation, and a deterministic two-stage color calibration to align the rendered 3DGS image to the robot's deployment camera. On a Unitree G1 humanoid robot, across three pick-and-place tasks of increasing whole-body difficulty and three VLA backbones (psi_0, pi_0.5, GR00T N1.6), a policy trained purely on LEGS data matches or exceeds one trained on human teleoperation demos on every experiment. It also outperforms a mesh-only simulation baseline that ablates the effect of the 3DGS background, showing that photorealistic rendering is a key enabler for synthetic data transfer. Humanoid motion is recorded independently of scene appearance in LEGS, allowing the same auto-generated demonstrations to be re-rendered under new backgrounds and object meshes--covering a new scene at more than 15x lower cost than teleoperation--to augment training data for robustness to scene variations. Under combined object-and-scene appearance shift, the policy trained on re-rendered LEGS-AUG data maintains task success while the baseline trained on teleoperation data fails entirely. Our project page is located at https://legsvla.github.io/.

AI.tldr: LEGS is a hybrid simulator that combines mesh foregrounds with 3D Gaussian Splatting backgrounds to generate synthetic training data for humanoid loco-manipulation, achieving performance matching or exceeding human-teleoperation-trained policies across multiple tasks and backbones.
AI.motivation: Human teleoperation data for humanoid loco-manipulation is expensive and difficult to collect, and prior simulator-fine-tuned VLA policies fail to transfer effectively to real-world tasks.
AI.method: LEGS composites mesh foregrounds (robot, objects, props) over photorealistic 3DGS backgrounds reconstructed from handheld captures; uses procedural motion-primitive generators for scalable labeled demonstrations; applies two-stage color calibration for domain alignment.
AI.result: On a Unitree G1 humanoid across three pick-and-place tasks and three VLA backbones (psi_0, pi_0.5, GR00T N1.6), LEGS-trained policies match or exceed human-teleoperation-trained baselines, outperform mesh-only simulations, and enable scene re-rendering at >15x lower cost.
AI.conclusion: Photorealistic rendering via 3DGS is a key enabler for synthetic data transfer in humanoid loco-manipulation; LEGS enables cost-effective, scalable training data generation that is robust to scene and object appearance shifts.

title: Through the PRISM: Principle-Aware, Interpretable, and Multi-Scale Evaluation of Visual Designs
summary: Effective visual communication stems from the harmony of multiple design principles, such as readability, contrast, alignment, overlap, and coherence, which collectively govern clarity and intent of the communicator. While human designers reason holistically over these principles, machine agents typically condense them into a single heuristic score, offering limited interpretability and diagnostic precision. To address this gap, we introduce PRISM (PRinciple-aware, Interpretable, and Structure-guided Design Modifications), a benchmark that systematically perturbs professional layouts from the Crello dataset along measurable design principles. The benchmark comprises 100K perturbed training samples and 10K perturbed validation designs, each isolating a specific principle violation for controlled analysis of multimodal reasoning about design quality. We show that models like Qwen-2.5-VL and GPT-4o-mini are largely insensitive to targeted principle degradations, whereas GPT-4o exhibits global awareness without fine-grained disentanglement. Building on these insights, we propose a multi-scale evaluation framework that integrates lightweight scorers for quantitative assessment, instruction-tuned vision-language models for localised feedback, and prompt-based methods for global reasoning. Our framework provides interpretable explanations of design failures. Using these localised insights, we show targeted refinements that improve layout quality. Together, PRISM and our framework lay the foundation for interpretable design-literate multimodal reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a benchmark of 110K perturbed graphic designs (from Crello dataset) that systematically violates specific design principles (readability, contrast, alignment, overlap, coherence).
